---
title: "Quarto Folding Examples"
author: "AI Collaborator"
date: "2026-06-15"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## Introduction

This file demonstrates the three primary ways to create collapsible folds inside a Quarto document.

---

## 1. Native Code Folding

Below is a Python code chunk that is folded by default.

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "👉 Click here to reveal the Python code"

import math

radius = 5
area = math.pi * (radius ** 2)
print(f"The area of the circle is {area:.2f}")
```

---

## 2. Collapsible Callout

Quarto callouts can be made collapsible with `collapse="true"`. They start folded
and expand on click.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📝 Click to expand this note

This content is hidden until you click the title. Callouts support full Markdown
inside them, including lists and code:

- First hidden point
- Second hidden point

```python
print("Even code blocks work inside a collapsed callout")
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 💡 A folded tip

Use `collapse="false"` (the default for some types) to have them open initially.
:::

---

## 3. Raw HTML `<details>` Fold

For arbitrary collapsible content, drop straight into HTML. This works in any
HTML output and is the most flexible option.

<details>
<summary>🔽 Click to reveal arbitrary content</summary>

This block is a native browser `<details>` element. You can put **any** Markdown
or HTML here — tables, images, math, etc.

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| 1        | 2        |
| 3        | 4        |

</details>

---

## 4. Images

### Remote image

A standard Markdown image pulled from the web, with a caption and sizing:

![The Quarto logo](https://quarto.org/quarto.png){width=300 fig-alt="Quarto logo"}

### Generated image (folded code)

The code that produces this plot is folded; only the resulting image shows by
default.

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "👉 Show the plotting code"
#| fig-cap: "A simple sine wave generated with matplotlib"

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
y = np.sin(x)

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 3))
plt.plot(x, y, color="#89b4fa")
plt.title("sin(x)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

---

## 5. Foldable Entire Sections

The previous folds only collapse a block. To fold a **whole section** — its
heading *and* every paragraph, list, code chunk and image under it — wrap the
section in a raw `<details>` element and put the heading text in the `<summary>`.

<details>
<summary><strong>📂 Section A — click to expand the entire section</strong></summary>

Everything between the `<summary>` and the closing `</details>` is hidden until
you click, including subheadings:

#### Subsection A.1

Full Markdown still works in here:

- A bullet
- Another bullet

```python
print("A code block living inside a folded section")
```

#### Subsection A.2

And even images:

![Quarto logo, folded away](https://quarto.org/quarto.png){width=200 fig-alt="Quarto logo"}

</details>

<details open>
<summary><strong>📂 Section B — starts expanded (note the `open` attribute)</strong></summary>

Add `open` to the `<details>` tag to have a section rendered expanded by default,
while still letting the reader collapse it.

</details>
